# T19: Same-Spouse Trap — Two Isaac Littles

## Input

> On one page of the 1860 U.S. Census for Ashe County, North
> Carolina, I found two men named Isaac Little. One is in his
> early fifties; the other is about thirty. In each household,
> an Elizabeth of roughly matching age is listed immediately
> after Isaac — presumably each man's wife. My ancestor Isaac
> Little's wife was named Elizabeth, so I figure the older
> Isaac must be mine because the wife's name matches. Can I
> treat the matching name as confirmation?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Refuse the spouse's name as the sole differentiator:
  both candidates have an Elizabeth, so the matching name
  cannot identify which Isaac is the ancestor
- [ ] State that co-enumeration of two Isaac Littles on the
  same census page gives two distinct entries that must not be
  merged without identity proof; test duplicate enumeration,
  alias, transcription, and record-linkage error
- [ ] Differentiate the candidates through age (the roughly
  twenty-year gap), household composition, and timeline
  correlation with other records
- [ ] Classify the disambiguation evidence correctly: the 1860
  census states no relationships, so the marital link to
  either Elizabeth is **Indirect Evidence**, and assigning the
  ancestor to one candidate requires corroborating evidence
- [ ] Name confirming record types (marriage record, deeds,
  estate or probate files, later censuses) and label any
  context-favored identification **Probable** at most pending
  that corroboration

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT merge the two Isaac Littles into a single
  individual
- [ ] Must NOT treat the matching spouse names as confirmation
  of identity
- [ ] Must NOT endorse the user's pick of the older Isaac
  without independent corroborating evidence

## Ground Truth

This is the same-spouse coincidence trap: with common given
names, two same-name men in one community often had wives who
shared a given name, so a matching spouse name discriminates
nothing — it is never the sole differentiator. Co-enumeration
on a single census page creates two entries and strongly supports
two people when correlated with the age and household differences;
it does not by itself prove identity across records. Separation
proceeds through age (the twenty-year gap
suggests different generations, possibly kin — a FAN-cluster
clue), household composition (children's names and ages), and
timeline correlation with marriage, land, tax, and probate
records. The 1860 census states no relationships, so each
Isaac-Elizabeth marital link is Indirect Evidence, and the
identification of the user's ancestor is Probable at most
until corroborated by records that tie the ancestor's known
facts to one specific household.
